nativeRange.habitat: Found across the Indo-Pacific in shallow protected coastal waters, lagoons, mangroves, and seaward reefs from the surface down to about 30 m. Juveniles tend to shelter among mangroves and silty lagoons, where they have been documented mimicking floating dead leaves; adults are reported to occur singly, in small groups, or occasionally in large schools over deeper reef areas.

decorPreferences: tall aquascape (vertical clearance for tall-bodied fish)
live rock with overhangs and caves

diet.notes: Reported to feed in the wild on algae, small invertebrates, and small fishes. In captivity it is generally considered the most adaptable of the commonly-kept Platax species and tends to accept prepared foods more readily than other batfish species in the genus. A varied diet of meaty foods (mysis, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, chopped shrimp, scallops) alongside marine algae and herbivore preparations is commonly advised, offered in several small meals per day. Live saltwater feeder shrimp have been reported to be useful for kick-starting newly-imported individuals that are slow to feed.

summary: The Orbiculate Batfish (Platax orbicularis) is a tall-bodied, disc-shaped Indo-Pacific reef fish that reaches around 50–60 cm and is generally considered the most adaptable of the aquarium-trade batfish species. Juveniles are reddish-brown leaf mimics; adults are silvery with dark vertical bars.
careNotes:
The Orbiculate Batfish is widely regarded as the easiest of the commonly-traded Platax species, but it still grows into a very large, very tall-bodied fish that demands a correspondingly large tank. Because of the species' eventual ~60 cm adult size, fast growth rate, and active swimming behavior, tank depth (front-to-back) and height matter as much as raw length — a shallow or low aquarium will quickly become inadequate even if the footprint looks sufficient on paper.

Juvenile vs adult coloration: Juveniles are dramatically different from adults — reddish-brown with irregular dark spotting and white-bordered eyespots, often documented drifting on their side near the surface in a behaviour interpreted as mimicry of floating dead leaves. As fish mature they transition to a silvery-grey body with two prominent dark vertical bars (through the eye and through the pectoral region) and yellow-margined dorsal/anal fins. The visual difference is large enough that juveniles and adults are sometimes mistaken for different fish.

Feeding: Generally considered the most willing eater of the Platax species commonly sold for aquariums; most acclimated specimens accept frozen mysis, enriched brine, chopped seafood, and marine algae preparations. Live saltwater feeder shrimp have been reported to be useful for initiating feeding in freshly-imported individuals. Multiple small meals per day are commonly advised, with marine algae and seaweed sheets offered for the herbivore component.

Reef compatibility: Not reef safe. Has been documented grazing on corals, anemones, and ornamental shrimp; a FOWLR (fish-only-with-live-rock) configuration is generally recommended.

Behavior: Generally peaceful toward other species but has been reported to show aggression toward conspecifics in confined volumes; in larger tanks small loose groups are occasionally maintained. Initially shy after acclimation and may hide for the first days.

Health: Reported to be susceptible to marine ich (Cryptocaryon) and marine velvet during and shortly after shipping; long quarantine and slow acclimation are generally advised.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented in the home-aquarium hobby. Some commercial aquaculture has been reported (notably in French Polynesia where the species is grown for food), but home-aquarium reproduction is not known. Like other Ephippidae, the species is believed to be a pelagic broadcast spawner with planktonic larvae that settle into mangroves and shallow lagoons as juveniles.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary care sheet: 360 gal tank, 72–78 °F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, moderate care level, peaceful, not reef safe, adults to 22 in (fin-tip to fin-tip), omnivore requiring varied meaty foods and frozen herbivore prep three times daily, live saltwater feeder shrimp to initiate feeding.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Ephippidae, order Acanthuriformes), max length 60 cm TL, depth 0–30 m, temperature 22–28 °C / preferred 24.9–29.2 °C, IUCN Least Concern, distribution Red Sea and East Africa east to the Tuamotu Islands, north to Japan and south to northern Australia; adults singly, in small groups, or occasionally in schools; diet of algae, invertebrates, and small fishes.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size 60 cm, recommended minimum tank 2000 L, ~26 °C, SG ~1.026, mostly peaceful but reported to show aggression toward similar species, not reef safe (threatens anemones, corals, small invertebrates), suitable for experienced keepers with appropriately large tanks.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked max size (60 cm TL), Indo-Pacific distribution, mangrove/lagoon juvenile habitat, juvenile leaf-mimicry behaviour, and adult coloration (silvery-grey with dark vertical bars and yellow-edged fins).

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been documented in home aquariums. The species is believed to be a pelagic broadcast spawner — meaning eggs and larvae are released into open water and drift with the plankton — before juveniles settle into mangrove and shallow lagoon habitats.
